attributes.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a 12-month history of increasingly troublesome aching in her legs, worse at the end of the day, associated with ankle oedema. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. She has very prominent bilateral superficial varicose veins along the distribution of the great saphenous vein. There is haemosiderin deposition and dry thin skin overlying the medial malleoli. Duplex ultrasound scanning confirms incompetence of both great saphenous veins at their origins, with several perforators in the thigh.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
Radiofrequency ablation is the most appropriate treatment in this case. It is a minimally invasive technique that provides effective long-term results for saphenous varicosities, comparable to traditional surgery but with reduced recovery time and less morbidity. It uses thermal energy to cause endothelial damage, vein wall collagen contraction, and eventual fibrosis of the vein.

Liquid sclerotherapy is more suitable for smaller, superficial varicose veins and spider veins. It involves injecting a sclerosing agent directly into the vein, causing it to collapse and eventually be reabsorbed by the body. While effective for smaller veins, it is not the best choice for the large saphenous veins described in this case.

Topical compression bandages are a conservative management option and can be helpful in managing symptoms of varicose veins. However, they do not address the underlying venous incompetence and are not a definitive treatment for this patient's condition. They may be used as an adjunct to other treatments or for patients who are not candidates for more invasive procedures.

Foam sclerotherapy can be effective for treating varicose veins, including larger ones. It involves injecting a foam sclerosant into the affected veins under ultrasound guidance. While it can be a good option in some cases, it has a higher mid-term recurrence rate of saphenous vein incompetence compared to radiofrequency ablation or surgical options.

Surgical ligation and stripping was traditionally the gold standard treatment for varicose veins. While effective, it is more invasive than newer techniques like radiofrequency ablation. It involves making incisions to tie off and remove the affected veins. This option is associated with longer recovery times and potentially more complications compared to minimally invasive techniques.

In summary, radiofrequency ablation offers the best balance of effectiveness and minimal invasiveness for this patient with significant great saphenous vein incompetence. It provides long-term results comparable to surgery but with faster recovery and less morbidity.
learningPoints: Radiofrequency ablation can provide effective long-term results for saphenous varicosities with minimal invasiveness and reduced recovery time compared to traditional surgery.
Duplex ultrasound is essential for confirming the diagnosis and planning treatment of varicose veins.
Chronic venous insufficiency can lead to skin changes such as haemosiderin deposition and lipodermatosclerosis.
references: Vemulapalli S, Parikh K, Coeytaux R, et al. Systematic review and meta-analysis of endovascular and surgical revascularization for patients with chronic lower extremity venous insufficiency and varicose veins. Am Heart J 2018;196:131-143.
Brittenden J, Cooper Dimitrova M, et al. Five-Year Outcomes of a Randomized Trial of Treatments for Varicose Veins. N Engl J Med 2019; 381:912-22.
